title: Demonstrating magnetic memory in iron-rhodium structures using a quantum diamond microscope
abstract: Iron-rhodium (FeRh) has a first-order phase transition near room temperature between antiferromagnetic (AFM) and ferromagnetic (FM) phases, making it a promising material for magnetic memory technologies like heat-assisted magnetic recording (HAMR). It has a comparatively sharper phase transition and lower writing temperature than alternative materials, implying less thermal engineering constraints and an increase in write/read head lifetime. Despite great effort, however, AFM-based magnetic memory using FeRh has not yet been realized. Here, we employ both wide-field and scanning nanoscale quantum diamond microscopes (QDMs) to image directly the magnetic field of a patterned FeRh thin film structure under ambient conditions, demonstrating a magnetic recording technique that is reliable and robust. We experimentally identify coupling between the Néel and magnetization vector directions; and also, that the magnetic orientation of the FM phase uniquely determines the Néel vector in the AFM phase, due to pinned uncompensated magnetic moments (UMMs) in the FeRh structure. Thus, the magnetic orientation is maintained when the system is cycled between AFM and FM phases, providing the foundation for a practical, AFM-based magnetic memory.
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures, to be submitted to Nature Materials

bite.hook: Quantum diamond microscopes have successfully demonstrated robust magnetic memory in iron-rhodium structures.
tldr: They investigated the viability of iron-rhodium (FeRh) structures for practical antiferromagnetic-based magnetic memory.
They used wide-field and scanning nanoscale quantum diamond microscopes to image magnetic fields during phase transitions.
They discovered that the magnetic orientation is preserved across temperature cycles due to pinned uncompensated magnetic moments.
bite.whyItMatters: Antiferromagnetic memory could make hard drives and computer storage much faster, smaller, and more energy-efficient. This work proves that iron-rhodium can reliably store data even when heated and cooled.
bite.eli5: Some materials change their magnetic state when heated. The researchers used a special diamond microscope to watch this happen and found that the material 'remembers' its magnetic direction even after being heated up and cooled back down.

glossary.shortDef: A magnetic state where neighboring atomic spins point in opposite directions, canceling each other out.
glossary.eli5Def: A material where tiny atomic magnets point in alternating opposite directions, so the material has no overall magnetic pull.

glossary.shortDef: A magnetic state where atomic spins align in the same direction, creating a strong external magnetic field.
glossary.eli5Def: A normal magnet, like the one on your fridge, where all the tiny atomic magnets line up to pull on iron.

glossary.shortDef: A vector describing the direction of magnetic alignment in an antiferromagnetic material.
glossary.eli5Def: An arrow that points along the line where the alternating magnets in an antiferromagnet are aligned.
